dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 for breathing difficulty at the Bus Station, 56-year-old female.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I can't catch my breath and my chest feels tight. Started smoking earlier, and things got worse.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and the date?
My name's Sarah. I'm not sure about the date.
Do you know where you are?
Yeah, I'm at the bus stop in Richmond.
What happened today?
I smoke a cigarette, started to get short of breath and chest pain, so I called 911.
Ma'am, we're going to do a couple of things at once to check how you're doing - is that okay with you?
Yeah, go ahead.
Your chest rhythm seems normal, and there's equal movement on both sides of your ribs. That's good to know.
Ma'am, we're going to take your blood pressure now.
Your blood pressure is high - 169 over 129. Let's keep an eye on it.
Your pulse is 125, which is fast.
Respiration rate is 20 per minute. Deep and steady.
Pulse ox reads at 96%. Let me patch in a nebulizer next.
Our monitor shows a sinus rhythm with tachycardia. We'll take a 12-lead now.
You mentioned chest pain and shortness of breath. Can you describe the chest pain more?
It's a 10 out of 10, thick and feels like it's pressing on my belly.
Does it get worse with any movement or actions?
Yeah, breathing is the big problem. Other things kind of make it worse.
Are you having any nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea?
No, I'm fine there.
Any urinary issues or blood in your urine or stool?
Not that I noticed.
Fever or cold symptoms lately?
No, I'm clean on that front.
What medications are you currently taking?
Prozac, Lithium, Albuterol, and metformin - also Glypizide, I think?
Do you have any allergies to medications or foods?
No known drug allergies.
What's your medical history?
COPD, arthritis, diabetes, and some mental health stuff - schizoaffective and bipolar.
What's the last thing you ate or drank?
I smoked a cigarette an hour ago.
Just to clarify, you were at the bus station waiting, and after smoking the cigarette, the symptoms started, right?
Yes, that's exactly when it happened.
We'll manage this using the respiratory distress protocol. These are signs of a COPD exacerbation or reactive airway.
Your skin is pink, warm, and dry. Let me shine a light in your eyes to check your pupils next.
Now I need to listen to your lungs. Can you take a deep breath in and out for me?
Her lung sounds have widespread wheezing and are pretty limited.
I need to check your neck for signs of difficulty. Can I look at it?
Go ahead.
I do not see JVD. Neck appears normal.
Feel around the abdomen with your hand as I press gently. It is soft but there's a large surgical scar down the middle.
What is the best way to describe your mental state right now?
I am. I know exactly what's going on.
And can you follow my finger when I move it side to side?
Sure, you can move it and I'll follow it.
Great. That tells us you are alert and oriented times four, with a GCS score of 15.
We're going to start a nebulizer treatment now. We'll combine albuterol and Atrovent to help your breathing.
Also, we'll fit you with oxygen via a mask at 6 liters per minute.
Let's take a blood sugar. It's normal at 116. No emergency readings.
Lastly, we're going to give you dexamethasone intravenously, and we'll start an IV in your left arm.
I'm placing an IV in your left arm using an 18-gauge catheter in an extremity vein.
I'll check your temperature with a tympanic thermometer. It reads 98.2°F. No fever signs here.
I'll run an ECG after this to check your heart rhythm.
How long has your chest pain been a 10 out of 10? Does anything help or make it worse?
It's been that bad since it started. Doesn't help, but breathing is better with the mask.
I'm going to check your blood pressure again now. It's come down to 137 over 78.
Pulse has also dropped to 109, which is still a bit fast but not as severe.
How would you rate your chest pain now on a scale of 0-10?
Still 10, I don't feel much change, but breathing is easier.
Would you like us to transport you to VCU ED?
Yes, please. I want to be assessed properly.
We are going to place you onto the stretcher now. All safety belts will be secured.
Seat belts are secured, and we're ready for transport. Let's begin heading to the hospital now.
Before we go, I'll let the hospital know we're on our way with a patient having COPD and reactive airway symptoms.
The 12-lead ECG was performed in the field and is available for review at the hospital.
I'll patch through the handoff report now while we move toward the ER.
Turnover report given to Dr. Ornato and the signing RN at VCU ED. Vital signs and protocol adherence are detailed.
Sarah, we're almost at the ER. They're expecting us and have a doctor ready. You're doing great.
Pulse ox is 93% now. We may need to adjust the oxygen flow to help it rise.
Still 20 per minute. Breathing is showing signs of improvement per the nebulizer.
One last check of her vitals before we enter the emergency room.
Blood pressure is steady at 118 over 72 now. Pulse is 108 and stable.
Sarah, we're here now. Ready to hand you over while you're on the stretcher if that's okay.
A turnover report has been transmitted to VCU ED. Sarah is alert and everything is ready for handoff.
She continues to wheeze on arrival, but says breathing is easier now.
Partial response is noted per EPCR.
The patient will be off the stretcher and onto the hospital bed once we enter.
All hospital departments are aware of our arrival, and the patient's details were passed in advance.
Sarah, we're here now. Ready to hand you over while you're on the stretcher if that's okay.
A turnover report has been transmitted to VCU ED. Sarah is alert and everything is ready for handoff.
The field impression was COPD exacerbation and non-cardiac chest pain. Protocols were followed fully.
Thank you, Sarah, for letting us help. The team at the hospital will be in charge now.
